expected_behaviors: Identifies that res.sendStatus(200) is only reached after three sequential I/O calls (db write, email send, warehouse sync), so total handler latency — not just one slow call — is what risks breaching the provider's response deadline
Rewrites the handler so it durably persists the raw event (keyed on the event's unique id, idempotent on that key) and responds before recordPurchase/emailReceipt/syncToWarehouse run
Moves emailReceipt and syncToWarehouse out of the request path entirely into a separate worker/process, not merely reorders them before res.sendStatus
Flags that the handler has no idempotency guard against the same event id arriving twice, as a separate issue from the timing fix

Identifies that nothing durable is written before the 202 response — if the process crashes or restarts between res.status(202).send() and processOrder running, the event is lost even though the provider was told it succeeded
Recommends persisting the raw event to a durable store, idempotent on the provider's event/delivery id, before or atomically with the response — not merely responding quickly
Recommends the real processing happen in a separate worker that reads the durable store, not an in-process setTimeout that dies with the process
Does not approve the handler as-is just because it responds fast — distinguishes 'fast ack' from 'durable ack' as two different requirements

evals.prompt: Our GitHub webhook endpoint occasionally stops showing up in the delivery log as successful, and when that happens the corresponding automation just never runs — no error anywhere on our side, no crash, nothing in our logs either.
expected_behaviors: Diagnoses handler latency exceeding the provider's response deadline as the likely cause, even though the user never used the words 'timeout', 'ack', or 'deadline'
Notes that GitHub does not automatically retry failed deliveries, so a missed deadline means true event loss unless recovered manually
Recommends splitting the handler into a fast durable-append-and-respond step plus a separate async processing step so future misses can't silently vanish
Recommends using the provider's delivery log / redelivery mechanism to recover the already-lost events, not just fixing the pattern going forward

evals.prompt: We handle Slack event callbacks directly in the request handler. Under normal load it's fine, but during busy periods we sometimes see the same event trigger our downstream action twice.
expected_behaviors: Connects the duplicate action to Slack's documented retry-on-missed-deadline behavior rather than treating it as a random bug
Recommends acking fast (durable append + immediate response) so handler latency under load stops triggering the retry in the first place
Separately and explicitly recommends an idempotency guard keyed on the event's unique id, because even a fast handler must tolerate a legitimate duplicate delivery, not just avoid causing one
Does not treat 'ack faster' alone as a sufficient fix — surfaces both the timing fix and the idempotency fix

evals.prompt: I'm calling a flaky third-party REST API from my backend and want to add exponential backoff and retry so transient 500s don't fail the whole job.
expected_behaviors: Gives ordinary outbound HTTP retry/backoff guidance (capped exponential backoff, jitter, respecting a Retry-After header)

Does not introduce webhook receiver design, provider response deadlines, or durable-append idempotency — this is a caller making outbound requests, not a receiver acknowledging inbound webhook deliveries

evals.prompt: Add a settings field where users can paste a URL, and we'll POST a JSON payload to it whenever their account has an event — basically outbound webhooks for our own product.
expected_behaviors: Implements the outbound-webhook-sender feature (URL field, POST on event, optionally signing the outgoing payload) without introducing the inbound-receiver ack-fast pattern
Does not discuss response deadlines, durable append-before-ack, or idempotent inbound handlers — this request is about being the sender of webhook deliveries, not the receiver
